1: Psalm 55 is a Maskil of David — a contemplative or instructional poem (maskîl: from śākal, to understand, to act wisely). The opening plea: הַאֲזִינָה אֱלֹהִים תְּפִלָּתִי (haʾăzînāh ʾĕlōhîm tĕpillātî, "Give ear to my prayer, O God") — ʾāzan (hiphil: to give ear, to incline the ear — a physical metaphor of tilting the ear toward the speaker). וְאַל-תִּתְעַלַּם מִתְּחִנָּתִי (wĕʾal-titʿallam mittĕḥinnātî, "do not hide yourself from my plea") — ʿālam (hithpael: to hide oneself, to conceal oneself): the same divine self-hiding as hester pānim — the God who could hear but turns away. Tĕḥinnāh: a supplication for grace, a plea for unearned mercy (from ḥānan: to be gracious).
2: הַקְשִׁיבָה לִּי וַעֲנֵנִי (haqshîbāh llî waʿănênî, "Pay attention to me and answer me") — qāshab (hiphil: to attend, to prick up the ears): the posture of alert listening. אָרִיד בְּשִׂיחִי (ʾārîd bĕśîḥî, "I am troubled in my lament") — rûd: to range about restlessly, to wander without rest (used of Cain in Gen 4:12 — "a fugitive and wanderer shall you be"). Śîaḥ: meditation, musing — the word connotes inward reflection that has become outward complaint (Ps 77:3: "I meditate and my spirit faints"). וְאָהִימָה (wĕʾāhîmāh, "and I am agitated") — hāmāh: to roar, to make noise (the same inner tumult of Ps 42:5 — the noisy, turbulent grief).
3: מִקּוֹל אוֹיֵב (miqqôl ʾôyêb, "because of the threatening voice of the enemy") — the qôl (voice/sound) of the enemy as a physical threat. מִפְּנֵי עָקַת רָשָׁע (mippĕnê ʿāqat rāshāʿ, "from the oppressive presence of the wicked") — ʿāqāh: pressure, oppression (rare — only here and Ps 66:11). כִּי-יָמִיטוּ עָלַי אָוֶן (kî-yāmîṭû ʿālay ʾāwen, "for they bring evil upon me") — māṭāh (hiphil: to cause to sway, to make fall, to bring down): the image of tilting a load until it falls — the enemy loading evil onto the Psalmist. ʾĀwen: trouble, harmful intent, iniquity.
4: לִבִּי יָחִיל בְּקִרְבִּי (libbî yāḥîl bĕqirbî, "My heart is writhing in anguish within me") — ḥûl (qal: to whirl, to writhe — the word for birth pangs, the churning of labor): the heart in the Psalmist's interior churning with the convulsive pain of crisis. וְאֵימוֹת מָוֶת נָפְלוּ עָלָי (wĕʾêmôt māwet nāpĕlû ʿālay, "the terrors of death have fallen on me") — ʾêmāh: dread, terror (from the paralyzing terror-root); māwet: death. The "terrors of death" as personified agents that descend on the Psalmist like an ambush.
5: יִרְאָה וָרַעַד יָבֹא בִי (yirʾāh wāraʿad yābōʾ bî, "Fear and trembling have come upon me") — yirʾāh: fear (the emotional experience of danger); raʿad: trembling (the physical manifestation). וַתְּכַסֵּנִי פַּלָּצוּת (wattĕkassênî pallāṣût, "and shuddering has covered me") — pallāṣût: horror, shuddering (a hapax legomenon — appears only here in the OT). The word suggests a total enveloping of the body in trembling dread — a unique word for a unique extremity of fear.
6: וָאֹמַר מִי-יִתֶּן-לִי אֵבֶר כַּיּוֹנָה (wāʾōmar mî-yittĕn-lî ʾêber kayyônāh, "And I said, 'If only I had wings like a dove!'") — yônāh: a dove — the bird of peace and swift, light flight. The dove-wing fantasy: to be able to rise above the crisis and escape. אָעוּפָה וְאֶשְׁכֹּנָה (ʾāʿûpāh wĕʾeshkōnāh, "I would fly away and find rest") — ʿûp: to fly away; shākan: to dwell, to rest (the verb of the Mishkān/Tabernacle). The longing for a place of settled dwelling after the chaos of persecution.
7: הִנֵּה אַרְחִיק נְדֹד אָלִין בַּמִּדְבָּר (hinnêh ʾarḥîq nĕdōd ʾālîn bammidbar, "Yes, I would flee far away; I would make my dwelling in the wilderness") — nādad: to flee, to wander (the fugitive's flight — Elijah fled to the wilderness in 1 Kgs 19:3-4 with the same impulse: sitting under a broom tree and asking to die). The wilderness (midbar) as the paradoxical refuge — the place of danger is preferred to the dangerous city. Selāh marks the pause on this vision of escape.
8: אָחִישָׁה מִפְלָט לִי מֵרוּחַ סֹעָה מִסַּעַר (ʾāḥîshāh miplaṭ lî mêrûaḥ sōʿāh missaʿar, "I would hurry to my refuge from the driving wind and the storm") — ḥûsh (hiphil: to hasten, to make speed): the urgency of the flight. Miplaṭ: a place of escape, a refuge (from pālaṭ: to escape, to slip away). Rûaḥ sōʿāh: a driving/sweeping wind (the storm-force that sweeps everything away); saʿar: a tempest. The metaphor shifts: the enemies are now a tempest that must be outrun.
9: בַּלַּע אֲדֹנָי פַּלַּג שְׂפָתֵימוֹ (ballaʿ ʾădōnāy pallag śĕpātêmô, "Confuse them, O Lord; split their tongues apart!") — bālaʿ (piel: to swallow, to confuse/confound — used of God "confounding" the enemies): the Babel-reversal prayer. At Babel, God divided the one language into many (bālal, Gen 11:7); now the Psalmist prays for the same confusion against the enemy's coordinated plotting. Pallag śĕpātêmô: "divide/split their lips" — the divided tongue that cannot coordinate, cannot scheme in concert. The enemies' strength is their unified violent counsel — splitting their speech destroys their collective threat.
10: יוֹמָם וָלַיְלָה יְסוֹבְבֻהָ עַל-חוֹמֹתֶיהָ (yômām wālaylāh yĕsôbĕbûhā ʿal-ḥômōtêhā, "Day and night they patrol its walls") — the city's walls patrolled not by defenders but by the forces of ʾāwen and ʿāmāl. וְאָוֶן וְעָמָל בְּקִרְבָּהּ (wĕʾāwen wĕʿāmāl bĕqirbāh, "iniquity and trouble are entrenched within it") — ʿāmāl: toil, trouble, misery (the word for the crushing labor of oppression). The city that should be protected by soldiers on its walls is instead circled by wickedness and guarded by misery.
11: הַוּוֹת בְּקִרְבָּהּ (hawwôt bĕqirbāh, "Ruin is at its center") — hawwāh: destruction, ruin, calamity (only here and a few places — a strong word for violent collapse). וְלֹא-יָמִישׁ מֵרְחֹבָהּ תֹּךְ וּמִרְמָה (wĕlōʾ-yāmîsh mêrĕḥōbāh tōk ûmirmāh, "oppression and fraud never leave its public squares") — māyish (hiphil: to remove, to cause to depart): the evil never departs. Tōk: oppression, fraud (rare word, possibly related to crushing); mirmāh: deceit, treachery. The city's rĕḥōb (broad open spaces, the marketplace and town square) is dominated by fraud — the public life corrupted throughout.
12: כִּי לֹא-אוֹיֵב יְחָרְפֵנִי וְאֶשָּׂא (kî lōʾ-ʾôyêb yĕḥārpênî wĕʾeśśāʾ, "For it is not an enemy who insults me — that I could endure") — ḥārap: to taunt, to reproach. The Psalmist makes the stunning claim: an enemy's reproach is bearable — expected, even. לֹא-מְשַׂנְאִי עָלַי הִגְדִּיל וְאֶסָּתֵר מִמֶּנּוּ (lōʾ-mĕśanʾî ʿālay higdîl wĕʾessātêr mimmennû, "nor is it someone who hates me who has risen up against me — from that I could hide") — sāthar (niphal: to hide oneself): one can avoid an openly hostile enemy. The betrayal's unique anguish: it comes from where defense was impossible because no defense was imagined as necessary.
13: וְאַתָּה אֱנוֹשׁ כְּעֶרְכִּי (wĕʾattāh ʾĕnôsh kĕʿerkî, "But it is you — a person like me, my peer") — ʾĕnôsh: a mortal human being (the word emphasizing human frailty); kĕʿerkî: "like my valuation/estimation" — my equivalent, my peer in social standing and worth. אַלּוּפִי וּמְיֻדָּעִי (ʾallûpî ûmĕyuddāʿî, "my companion, my close friend") — ʾallûp: a close associate, an intimate companion (originally "chief" but used for a personal confidant); mĕyuddāʿî: my intimate/familiar one (from yādaʿ: the deepest knowing). This is the vocabulary of intimate covenant friendship violated — parallel to Ps 41:9 (ʾîsh shĕlômî / ʾōkêl laḥmî) and to John 13:18.
14: אֲשֶׁר יַחְדָּו נַמְתִּיק סוֹד (ʾăsher yaḥdāw namtîq sôd, "We shared deep, sweet counsel together") — mātaq (hiphil: to make sweet, to sweeten); sôd: intimate counsel, the secret of private conversation (the word for the divine council in Jer 23:18 — "who has stood in the counsel of the LORD?"). The shared secret is sweet; its betrayal is correspondingly bitter. בְּבֵית אֱלֹהִים נְהַלֵּךְ בְּרָגֶשׁ (bĕbêt ʾĕlōhîm nĕhallêk bĕrāgesh, "together we walked in God's house among the festival crowd") — rāgesh: throng, crowd (only here — the festival multitude moving in joyful procession). The betrayal is set against the background of shared worship — the deepest form of covenant fellowship.
15: יַשִּׁי מָוֶת עֲלֵימוֹ (yashshî māwet ʿălêmô, "Let death seize them!") — nāshāʾ (or shāʾāh: to deceive, to lead astray — the reading is disputed): "let death ambush/deceive them." יֵרְדוּ שְׁאוֹל חַיִּים (yêrĕdû shĕʾôl ḥayyîm, "let them go down to Sheol alive") — the most intense imprecation: descent to Sheol while still living — the fate of Korah and his rebellion (Num 16:30-33: "the earth opened its mouth and swallowed them up... and they went down alive into Sheol"). The imprecation invokes the Korah precedent as the appropriate judgment for covenant betrayal.
16: אֲנִי אֶל-אֱלֹהִים אֶקְרָא (ʾănî ʾel-ʾĕlōhîm ʾeqrāʾ, "As for me, I will call upon God") — the decisive "I" (ʾănî) that counters the "they." The structural pivot: the enemies seek the Psalmist's death; the Psalmist calls upon God. וַיהוָה יוֹשִׁיעֵנִי (waYHWH yôshîʿênî, "and the LORD will rescue me") — yāshaʿ (hiphil: to rescue, to save): the confident future claim in the face of present danger. The trust is not a feeling but a theological claim: YHWH is the one who saves.
17: עֶרֶב וָבֹקֶר וְצָהֳרַיִם אָשִׂיחָה וְאֶהֱמֶה (ʿereb wābōqer wĕṣāhŏrayim ʾāśîḥāh wĕʾehĕmeh, "Evening and morning and at noon I cry out my lament and sigh") — the three times of daily prayer: evening, morning, noon. Daniel prays at these same three times (Dan 6:10 — three times daily with windows open toward Jerusalem). This verse suggests a pre-Danielic practice of structured daily prayer in which the Psalmist maintains continuous covenant communion even in crisis. Ʃîaḥ (lament-meditation) and hāmāh (inner sighing-roaring): both complaint and release. וַיִּשְׁמַע קוֹלִי (wayyishmaʿ qôlî, "and he hears my voice") — the confident present-tense hearing: not "he will hear" but "he hears" — the continuous divine attention.
18: פָּדָה בְשָׁלוֹם נַפְשִׁי מִקֲּרָב-לִי (pādāh bĕshālôm napshî miqqĕrab-lî, "He ransoms me safely from the battle I am in") — pādāh: to ransom, to redeem (the redemption vocabulary of Exod 6:6 — God ransoming Israel from Egypt). Bĕshālôm: in wholeness, in peace — ransomed with no part missing. כִּי-בְרַבִּים הָיוּ עִמָּדִי (kî-bĕrabbîm hāyû ʿimmādî, "for they are many who oppose me") — the many-to-one odds that make the divine rescue more remarkable: the LORD against the many, on behalf of the one.
19: יִשְׁמַע אֵל וְיַעֲנֵם (yishmaʿ ʾêl wĕyaʿănêm, "God will hear and afflict them") — ʿānāh (piel: to afflict, to oppress — the same word used of Egypt's affliction of Israel in Exod 1:11). וְיֹשֵׁב קֶדֶם סֶלָה (wĕyōshêb qedem selāh, "the one who is enthroned from of old") — qedem: the ancient time, the primordial past: God enthroned from of old — the eternal divine sovereignty that makes the judge's verdict certain. אֲשֶׁר אֵין חֲלִיפוֹת לָמוֹ (ʾăsher ʾên ḥălîpôt lāmô, "because they show no sign of change") — ḥălîpāh: a change, an exchange, an alternation: the enemies have no ḥălîpôt (no changes, no repentance). The hardened heart as the condition that makes judgment inevitable.
20: שָׁלַח יָדָיו בִּשְׁלֹמָיו (shālaḥ yādāyw bishĕlōmāyw, "He raised his hand against those at peace with him") — the subject returns to the betrayer of vv12-14: the one who "stretched out his hand" against shĕlōmāyw (those who were in covenant-peace with him). חִלֵּל בְּרִיתוֹ (ḥillêl bĕrîtô, "he broke his covenant") — ḥālal (piel: to profane, to desecrate): not merely breaking a contract but desecrating the sacred bond of covenant (bĕrît). The covenant-violation as a sacrilegious act.
21: חָלְקוּ מַחֲמָאֹת פִּיו (ḥāqĕlû maḥămāʾōt pîw, "His words were smoother than butter") — māḥāʾ/maḥămāʾōt: butter (the sense of soft, smooth richness — from mōʾ/ḥāmāʾ, fresh cream). Ḥālaq (qal: to be smooth, to be slippery): smooth speech as the weapon of deception (the same root as ḥelqāh — smooth ground, flattery). וּקְרָב לִבּוֹ (ûqĕrāb libbô, "yet war was in his heart") — the lethal contrast: the exterior is butter; the interior is war (qĕrāb: battle, combat). רַכּוּ דְבָרָיו מִשֶּׁמֶן (rakkû dĕbārāyw mishshemen, "his speech was softer than oil") — shemen: olive oil — the liquid that smooths and soothes. וְהֵמָּה פְתִחוֹת (wĕhêmmāh pĕtîḥôt, "but they were drawn swords") — pāttaḥ (pual: drawn/unsheathed): the soft speech concealing the drawn blade — the most dangerous enemy is the one who attacks with gentleness.
22: הַשְׁלֵךְ עַל-יְהוָה יְהָבְךָ (hashlêk ʿal-YHWH yĕhābĕkā, "Throw your burden onto the LORD") — shālak (hiphil imperative: to throw, to cast): a decisive physical action — the violent tossing of a burden. Yĕhāb: your burden (a rare word — found also in the Aramaic cognate, meaning what is given/assigned to you: your God-given portion, even when it is heavy). The command is not "trust" but "throw" — a more active, urgent gesture. וְהוּא יְכַלְכְּלֶךָ (wĕhûʾ yĕkalkĕlekā, "and he will sustain you") — kûl (pilpel: to sustain, to provide for, to bear up): the same word used of Joseph sustaining his family (Gen 47:12). Peter cites this verse in 1 Pet 5:7: "casting all your anxiety on him, because he cares for you." לֹא-יִתֵּן לְעוֹלָם מוֹט לַצַּדִּיק (lōʾ-yittên lĕʿôlām môṭ laṣṣaddîq, "he will never let the righteous be shaken") — māṭ: to totter, to slip — the righteous one preserved from the instability that threatens.
23: וְאַתָּה אֱלֹהִים תּוֹרִדֵם לִבְאֵר שַׁחַת (wĕʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm tôrîdêm libʾêr shaḥat, "But you, O God, will bring them down into the pit of ruin") — bĕʾêr shaḥat: the pit of destruction (the Sheol imagery — the underground pit that swallows the wicked). אַנְשֵׁי דָמִים וּמִרְמָה (ʾanshê dāmîm ûmirmāh, "men of bloodshed and treachery") — the double accusation: violent and deceitful. לֹא-יֶחֱצוּ יְמֵיהֶם (lōʾ-yeḥĕṣû yĕmêhem, "will not live out half their days") — ḥāṣāh: to halve, to divide in two: cut off in the middle of life. וַאֲנִי אֶבְטַח-בָּךְ (waʾănî ʾebṭaḥ-bāk, "but I — I will trust in you") — the emphatic personal pronoun (wāʾănî: but as for me) contrasting the fate of the wicked with the security of the trusting one. The psalm ends with the naked word of trust: bāṭaḥ.
